description: Building a dystopian short story concept

turns.content: I want to write a short story about a city where emotions are illegal. Can you help me develop the concept?

Rich premise with many directions. The first thing to figure out is the mechanism — how does the city enforce this? Option A: technological enforcement (emotion-scanning checkpoints, chemical suppressants in water, neural chips) — sci-fi thriller feel. Option B: social enforcement (neighbours report neighbours, showing emotion is shameful, a culture of performed numbness) — literary and psychological. The second big choice: is your protagonist someone who has suppressed their emotions and is starting to feel again, or someone who has always felt and has been hiding?
I like Option B — social enforcement. And the protagonist has always felt things but is good at hiding it. She's a teenager.

Excellent choices. A teenager who is a skilled actor of numbness is compelling — adolescence is already about performance and fitting in, so the stakes feel personal and universal simultaneously. The conflict writes itself: what happens when she encounters someone else who is also secretly feeling? A few things to decide: what is the inciting incident — what specifically cracks her facade? And what does the city call the enforcement? You will want a word for emotional transgression that feels organic to your world. Something clinical, like 'affect disorder' or 'sensitivity breach.'
I love 'affect disorder.' Can you write the opening paragraph?

Here is a draft:

The city measured stillness in the lines of a face. Mara had learned young that the safest expression was no expression — lips level, eyes focused at a point just past the person speaking to you, hands at your sides with fingers neither clenched nor spread. She had practised in mirrors until the glass looked back at something nearly machine-like, and the sight of herself had stopped producing any feeling at all. That, she understood, was the point. At sixteen, she had been cited only twice for affect disorder: once at her mother's funeral (a momentary breach, three days of compulsory recalibration), and once at age nine when she had laughed at a bird falling off a branch. The memory of that laugh had kept her careful ever since.

Want me to continue, or adjust the tone?
